coredata.dc:title: Novel dual-emission fluorescence imprinted sensor based on Mg, N-CDs and metal-organic frameworks for rapid and smart detection of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Lidong
Hu, Wei
Pei, Fubin
Du, Bin
Tong, Zhaoyang
Mu, Xihui
Xia, Mingzhu
Wang, Fengyun
Liu, Bing
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and real-time detection of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol (TNP) is of great importance for the living environment and human health. Herein, we constructed an innovative ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor with fast response and high selectivity based on magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Mg, N-CDs) and chromium telluride quantum dots (r-CdTe) self-assembled in zirconium-based metal organic frameworks (UiO-66) combined with imprinted polymers for the detection of TNP. In the protocol, the introduction of UiO-66 with large specific surface area and porosity using as carrier material significantly enhanced the mass transfer rate, which improved the sensitivity of the Mg, N-CDs/r-CdTe@UiO-66@MIP (LHU@MIP). And the Mg, N-CDs with high quantum yields and r-CdTe were selected as fluorescence emitting elements to yield fluorescence signal, achieving signal amplification. The dual-channel strategy enabled the sensor to not only display a fast fluorescence response, but also generate a dual-response signal under the action of internal filtering effect (IFE). Combining these advantages, the LHU@MIP had a wide linear range (1–100 μM), good detection sensitivity (0.56 μM), and a distinct color change (from blue to pink). Meanwhile, for accurate on-site analysis, we designed a portable smart sensing platform with a color recognizer application. The smartphone enabled visual sensing of TNP by capturing fluorescent images and converting them into digital values. More importantly, the platform was successfully utilized for the analysis of TNP in the simulated actual samples with considerable results. Therefore, the developed platform was characterized by low cost, portability, ideal specificity, and provided a strategy for on-site monitoring of TNP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mg
N-CDs
Metal organic frameworks
Dual-emission fluorescence
Molecularly imprinted polymers
6-Trinitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TNP in river water, tap water and soil
Smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing for real-time TNP detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian ( SKLNBC2022-06 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of UiO-66 (A), LHU@MIP (B), LHU@NIP (C). The corresponding elemental mappings of LHU@MIP (D). (E) XRD patterns of UiO-66, LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP. (F) FTIR spectra of Mg, N-CDs, UiO-66, LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP.
XPS survey spectra of Mg, N-CDs (A). High-resolution XPS spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) O1s and (E) Mg1s for Mg, N-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of LHU@MIP, LHU@NIP and LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP combined with TNP (20 μM). (B) The fluorescence excitation spectra (a) and emission spectra (b) of the LHU@MIP, UV–vis absorption spectra of TNP (c).

Fluorescence spectra of (A) LHU@MIP and (B) LHU@NIP under different concentrations of TNP (excited at 350 nm, TNP concentrations: 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100 μM). The Stern-Volmer plots of (C) LHU@MIP and (D) LHU@NIP. (Error bar = SD, n = 3).

(A) Selectivity of the LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP with TNP and its analogues (the concentration is 100 μM). (B) and (C) Fluorescence responses of the LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP toward TNP and other interfered ions (100 μM and 4 mM). (Error bar = SD, n = 3).

Schematic diagram of using a smartphone to detect TNP and fluorescence images captured by smartphone of LHU@MIP upon the addition of different concentrations of TNP and the corresponding linear relation between R/B and TNP concentrations (TNP concentrations: 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80 μM).
Schematic illustration of synthetic process of LHU@MIP and smartphone-assisted detection of TNP.
Determination of TNP in simulated real samples by the proposed LHU@MIP.
Detection of TNP in simulated real samples by smartphone-based sensing platform.

head.ce:title: Novel dual-emission fluorescence imprinted sensor based on Mg, N-CDs and metal-organic frameworks for rapid and smart detection of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing, 102205, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian, Beijing 102205, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and real-time detection of 2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol (TNP) is of great importance for the living environment and human health. Herein, we constructed an innovative ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor with fast response and high selectivity based on magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Mg, N-CDs) and chromium telluride quantum dots (r-CdTe) self-assembled in zirconium-based metal organic frameworks (UiO-66) combined with imprinted polymers for the detection of TNP. In the protocol, the introduction of UiO-66 with large specific surface area and porosity using as carrier material significantly enhanced the mass transfer rate, which improved the sensitivity of the Mg, N-CDs/r-CdTe@UiO-66@MIP (LHU@MIP). And the Mg, N-CDs with high quantum yields and r-CdTe were selected as fluorescence emitting elements to yield fluorescence signal, achieving signal amplification. The dual-channel strategy enabled the sensor to not only display a fast fluorescence response, but also generate a dual-response signal under the action of internal filtering effect (IFE). Combining these advantages, the LHU@MIP had a wide linear range (1–100 μM), good detection sensitivity (0.56 μM), and a distinct color change (from blue to pink). Meanwhile, for accurate on-site analysis, we designed a portable smart sensing platform with a color recognizer application. The smartphone enabled visual sensing of TNP by capturing fluorescent images and converting them into digital values. More importantly, the platform was successfully utilized for the analysis of TNP in the simulated actual samples with considerable results. Therefore, the developed platform was characterized by low cost, portability, ideal specificity, and provided a strategy for on-site monitoring of TNP.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) mimick the high selectivity of the antibody, which can adsorb analytes by leaving a lot of imprinted cavities that match with templates in structure, shape and size [1]. Until now, MIPs have the advantages of high sensitivity, excellent specificity and simplicity of synthesis [2,3], and have been used in the fields of sensing [4], environmental science [5], food safety [6], biomedicine [7], etc. However, the synthesis of MIPs using bulk polymerization usually suffer from difficult elution and limited imprinting efficiency. To overcome the affects, it is important to find appropriate supporter for molecularly imprinted sensor.

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are formed by the assembly of multidentate organic ligands with metal ions or metal clusters through coordination bonds [8]. With extremely high surface areas, uniform pore sizes, strong chemical tailoring ability, and good thermal stability [9,10]. Due to the elasticity of the MOFs composition, MOFs were used in gas adsorption/storage [11], heterogeneous catalysis [12], luminescence [13], and carrier functions [14]. In addition, MOFs can be used as nanomaterial scaffolds loaded with organic probes for the design of optical and fluorescent chemical sensors for heavy metal detection [15,16]. Furthermore, MOFs have been widely used in imprinted fluorescence sensors. For example, Chen et al. [17] fabricated a imprinted fluorescence sensor by adopting zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) as the supporter for sensitive detection of trypsin. To realize selective and visual detection of HVA, Wu et al. [18] proposed a tricolor fluorescence imprinted sensor by embedding NH2-MIL-88 into the imprinted layer. Researches show that UiO-66 is a typical zirconium-based MOF, which has been used as a support material for imprinted fluorescence sensor due to its porous structure, good thermal stability and ideal water stability [19]. In addition, its high specific surface area and superior signal transduction capabilities, provided more imprinted sites, which further enhanced the sensitivity of sensor. Hu et al. [19] prepared fluorescence sensors using UiO-66 for detecting bovine hemoglobin, which exhibited good sensitivity. However, the development of MOFs-based fluorescence sensors still suffer from the limitation such as poor sensitivity, which was ascribed to the low fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY) of the luminescent quantum dots [20]. Compared with Carbon dots, magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Mg, N-CDs) have high FLQY (33%) [21] and have been exploited in imprinted fluorescent sensor. For instance, Hu et al. [22] prepared a molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensor utilizing Mg, N-CDs for detecting oxytetracycline, which improved the fluorescence efficiency and fabricated sensitive fluorescent probe by using Mg, N-CDs for the analysis of the tetracycline in animal-derived foods [23].

2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol (TNP), commonly called as picric acid, is a nitroaromatic hydrocarbon. It is widely used as a precursor for the synthesis of explosives and pharmaceuticals [24–27]. TNP is often used in the manufacture of dyes and pesticides, and is also used as a surgical astringent in medicine. However, TNP is highly toxic and strongly irritating. Prolonged exposure will cause headache, dizziness, nausea and vomiting, loss of appetite, diarrhea and other symptoms. In addition, TNP is a strong oxidizing agent, it is easy to violent explosion by friction or vibration. It also corrodes metals and the metal salt generated is extremely sensitive to vibration or heat. Therefore. The detection of TNP is of great significance in the fields of national security, environmental protection, food monitoring and biomedicine. Various TNP detection methods have been developed, including mass spectrometry [28,29], high performance liquid chromatography [30] and gas chromatography [31]. In addition, several electrochemical methods for detecting TNP have been described [32,33]. However, most of these methods suffered from time consuming, expensive instruments and complex pre-processing process. Therefore, it is important to develop sensors for sensitively and rapidly detecting TNP. The molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensors have received much attentions for the merits of low instrument cost, high sensitivity and high selectivity [34]. Currently, Wang et al. [35] prepared a magnetic fluorescent composite material by wrapping a imprinted layer on the surface of the conucleus, which provided a new strategy for detecting TNP. Li et al. [36] prepared a ratiometric fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor for visual recognition and detection of TNP. However, imprinted fluorescence sensors based on MOFs still have drawbacks in practical applications, such as low portability. Moreover, compared to single wavelength, the fluorescence sensors with two emissions provided more accurate analysis results by eliminating interferences such as background fluorescence and environmental interference [37,38]. Although dual-emission fluorescence imprinted sensor for the detection of TNP has been investigated [36], it used the large equipment, which was adverse to on-site and real-time detection of TNP. Therefore, the development of portable devices based on MOFs is important for the real-time monitoring of TNP.

Based on the above considerations, we proposed a facile and efficient dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence strategy for TNP detection by encapsulating Mg, N-CDs and r-CdTe in UiO-66 combined with molecularly imprinted polymers. UiO-66 with large surface area and porous structure was served as a supporter, which offered more imprinted sites. r-CdTe have strong anti-interference ability. And Mg, N-CDs with high FLQY as fluorophore, were loaded on the surface of UiO-66, which increased the sensitivity of the LHU@MIP. Moreover, UiO-66 can suppress aggregation of the Mg, N-CDs, thereby achieving signal amplification. The prepared LHU@MIP displayed excellent selectivity and sensitivity to the TNP. In the presence of TNP, the blue fluorescence at 446 nm (Mg, N-CDs) and the red fluorescence at 660 nm (r-CdTe) were effectively quenched due to the internal filtering effect (IFE). Here, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on this strategy enabled the LHU@MIP to display a double response signal accompanied by fluorescence color evolution from blue to pink, which enabled rapid response and visual detection of TNP. In addition, considering the requirement of on-site quantification in practical applications, a portable smartphone-integrated optical device was further developed by combining the proposed dual-emission LHU@MIP, which provided a new idea for TNP detection in practice. Fortunately, the portable equipment achieved the onsite analysis of TNP in actual samples with satisfactory results.
Experiments sections were placed in supporting information.

As illustrated in Scheme 1 , a fluorescence sensor with core-shell structure was prepared by sol-gel method. UiO-66 was synthesized by using phenylboronic acid and zirconium chloride as reaction precursors. Meanwhile, Mg, N-CDs with high quantum yields (39.95%) were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method and r-CdTe was prepared by refluxing at 100 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, the polymerized process was performed based on Mg, N-CDs and r-CdTe (fluorophores), UiO-66 (supporter), TNP (template), 3-aminopropyrtriethoxysilane (APTES) (functional monomer) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) (cross-linker). After removal of TNP, the specific recognition cavities of LHU@MIP for the TNP in size and shape were gained. And the utilization of UiO-66 provided a beneficial carrier for the capsulation of Mg, N-CDs and offered imprinted sites, which was good for enhancing imprinting efficiency. At the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks at 446 nm and 660 nm of LHU@MIP were attributed to Mg, N-CDs and r-CdTe, respectively. The fluorescence emission peaks at 446 nm and 660 nm were quenched significantly after the addition of TNP. Therefore, the different fluorescence responses of LHU@MIP can be used to detect TNP in real samples.

The morphologies and structures of Mg, N-CDs, r-CdTe, UiO-66, LHU@MIP and Mg, N-CDs/r-CdTe@UiO-66@NIP (LHU@NIP) were investigated through transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. S1A, the Mg, N-CDs exhibited a good dispersion and uniform size with an average size of 2 nm, The TEM image of r-CdTe shown in Fig. S1B exhibited that the diameter of r-CdTe QDs was about 4 nm with uniform size. As depicted in Fig. 1 A, the image of the UiO-66 microcrystal was classic rhombic octahedron with a mean size of 80 nm. The average sizes of LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP portrayed in Fig. 1B and C were about 150 nm, which were larger than that of UiO-66 and demonstrated the successful preparation of the materials. The EDS spectrum (Fig. S2) and the elements mapping (Fig. 1D) denoted that the characteristic elements (Mg, N, Cd, Te, Zr, Si) were contained in the imprinted polymers, and conformed the formation of LHU@MIP.

The crystal structures of UiO-66, LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP were investigated by using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1E, the peaks of UiO-66 show at 2θ = 7.34°, 2θ = 8.48° and 2θ = 25.7° were similar to the reported reference [39], which confirmed the successful synthesis of UiO-66 with high crystallinity. Additionally, the peaks of LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP were different from UiO-66. The characteristic diffraction peaks (2θ = 23°) of LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP were attributed to amorphous SiO2, which was consistent with the previous report [40]. The results demonstrated the successful preparation of imprinted layer.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was utilized to verify the surface functional groups of Mg, N-CDs, UiO-66, LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP. As depicted in Fig. 1F, the peak of Mg, N-CDs at 1577 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of –COO and the peak at 1400 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration CO. The peaks at 1102 cm−1 and 854 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of C–N and bending vibration of N–H, respectively [21]. The results demonstrated the successful formation of Mg, N-CDs. The characteristic peaks of UiO-66 at 1584 cm−1 and 1397 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching modes of carboxylate group, and the peaks showed at 664 cm−1 and 747 cm−1 were attributed to Zr–O [41]. These results showed that the UiO-66 was synthesized successfully. As illustrated in Fig. 1F, due to the presence of aminopropyl of APTES, the peak at 1644 cm−1 was considered to be the stretching vibration of N–H. The peak at 1046 cm−1 belonged to the asymmetric Si–O–Si stretching [42]. And, the peaks at 766 cm−1 and 450 cm−1 were associated with the Si–O vibrations and Si–O–Si bending vibrations, respectively [43]. The results indicated successful preparation of the imprinted silica layer. And the results manifested that LHU@MIP was prepared successfully.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of Mg, N-CDs was displayed in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2A displayed the full spectrum of XPS of Mg, N-CDs, including four elements, C, N, O and Mg. Among them, the C1s XPS spectra showed peaks at 284.4 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.1 eV ( Fig. 2B), which were ascribed to C–C/CC, C–O and CO, respectively [44]. The N1s XPS spectra showed peaks of N–C (399.5 eV), N–H (400.7 eV), and N–O (401.5 eV) belonging to N–C, N–H, and N–O, respectively ( Fig. 2C). The O1s XPS spectra showed peaks of CO (531.2 eV) and C–O (533.2 eV) ( Fig. 2D). The Mg1s spectrum showed a peak at 1303.7 eV ( Fig. 2E). These results indicated that the Mg and N elements were successfully doped to form Mg, N-CDs.

The fluorescence intensity changes of LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP combing to TNP was investigated (Fig. 3 A) to assess the feasibility of the method. Compared with LHU@NIP, LHU@MIP presented more conspicuous fluorescence intensity changes due to the strong affinity between LHU@MIP and TNP. The indicated that the as-developed nanosensor had good feasibility for the detection of TNP. The mechanism of dual-response fluorescence quenching of TNP was researched. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, the UV absorption of TNP was at 350 nm and the emission of LHU@MIP was in the range from 410 nm to 750 nm. There was no overlap between the UV absorption of TNP and the emission of LHU@MIP, excluding the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). However, the fluorescence excitation spectrum of LHU@MIP overlapped significantly with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of TNP. The results preliminarily indicated that the fluorescence quenching of LHU@MIP was induced by internal filtering effect (IFE). To further validate the quenching principle, we also verified the correlation by measuring the fluorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence lifetime of the sensing system shows a slight fluctuation from 6.83 ns to 6.79 ns, which further confirmed that the quenching mechanism of the nanosensor was IFE [45].

The amounts of APTES and TEOS were optimized to obtain optimal imprinted efficiency. The results were displayed in Fig. S4A and S4B. The amounts of functional monomers has a large influence on the recognition properties of polymers. A low amount of cross-linking agent will lead to the collapse of the blot layer during elution, and a high amount of cross-linking agent will lead to the failure of TNP to elute during elution. To obtain the optimal sensing performance of LHU@MIP, the pH of the solvent and the response time were optimized. The influence of pH of LHU@MIP for TNP was investigated in the pH ranges of 5.0–10.0. As seen in Fig. S4C, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was highest at pH = 8.0. Excessive acidity or alkalinity will destroy the structure of the LHU@MIP. Therefore, pH = 8.0 was used for subsequent experiments. Meanwhile, the ratio of the two fluorescence sources was further optimized for optimal response and visual detection of TNP by bicolor fluorescence sensing system. The response time of LHU@MIP for TNP was depicted in Fig. S4D. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of LHU@MIP reached stability rapidly within 1.0 min. The rapidly response time of the fluorescent sensor was attributed to the larger surface area of UiO-66. And due to the specific imprinted sites of TNP, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of LHU@MIP was higher than LHU@NIP. To improve the fluorescence signal readout effect, the ratio of 10 mg:800 μL (Mg, N-CDs: r-CdTe) was chosen for the preparation of LHU@MIP (Fig. S4E).

Under the different concentrations of TNP, the fluorescence emissions of the materials were studied by investigating LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A–B, with the increase of TNP concentrations, the fluorescence emission peaks at 446 nm and 660 nm decreased gradually. The fluorescence intensity of LHU@MIP changed more rapidly than the fluorescence intensity of LHU@NIP when the TNP concentrations increased from 1 μM to 100 μM. As shown in Fig. 4C–D, within 1–100 μΜ, the values ((F660/F446)/(F660/F446)0) of LHU@MIP and TNP concentrations showed a good linear relationship. The slopes of the corresponding standard linear equations of LHU@MIP (y = 0.9872 + 0.0031 x, R2 = 0.992) and LHU@NIP (y = 0.9851 + 0.0011 x, R2 = 0.973). And the limit of detection (LOD) acquired by the standard 3σ/k [46] was 0.56 μM. Interestingly, LHU@MIP showed more abundant color changes (blue to pink) for TNP than LHU@NIP, manifesting that LHU@MIP had higher selectivity to TNP due to the specific binding sites. Imprinting factor (IF) is commonly used to assess the properties of MIPs materials. The IF was determined as 2.82 according to the report [22]. And the result denoted the better recognition ability between LHU@MIP and TNP.

For comparison, the fluorescence response ((F660/F446)/(F660/F446)0) of Mg, N-CDs/r-CdTe@MIP (LH@MIP) to TNP was studied in Fig. S5. Obviously, as the concentration of TNP increases, the fluorescence response of LH@MIP showed a linear correlation in the range of 2–70 μM. However, the LOD of LHU@MIP was lower than LH@MIP (1.58 μΜ). And LHU@MIP had a wider linear range of 1–100 μM and displayed significant color changes, which derived from the high porosity and large surface area of UiO-66. UiO-66, as imprinted supporter, offered more imprinted sites on the surface of LHU@MIP, which improved the ability of LHU@MIP to recognize TNP. The results demonstrated that UiO-66 was of great vital to improve the sensitivity of TNP.

To certify the excellent fluorescence properties of the proposed LHU@MIP probes, we performed controlled experiments on LHU@MIP and N-CDs/r-CdTe@UiO-66@MIP. LHU@MIP and N-CDs/r-CdTe@UiO-66@MIP were exposed to a series of different concentrations of TNP, and their adsorption capacity to TNP was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F660/F446)/(F660/F446)0) of the polymers increased dramatically with increasing TNP concentration (1–100 μM). However, at the same TNP concentration, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of LHU@MIP was higher than that of N-CDs/r-CdTe@UiO-66@MIP (Fig. S6A). The reason for the above phenomenon was that Mg, N-CDs as fluorophores enabled the LHU@MIP sensors to have superior optical properties (e.g., high quantum yields) and exhibit higher fluorescence response to TNP, which leaded to the amplification of the signals. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of Mg, N-CDs is stronger than that of N-CDs (Fig. S6B). The results indicated that the high quantum yields of quantum dots can improve the sensitivity of quantum dot-based sensors.

LHU@MIP and LHU@NIP towards TNP and nine analogues (2-nitrophenol, 3-nitrophenol, 4-nitrophenol, catechol, resorcinol, hydroquinone, 2, 4-dinitroaniline, phenol, 2, 4-dinitrophenol) were investigated to evaluate the selectivity of imprinted fluorescence sensors (Fig. 5 ). Compared with other analogues, only TNP generated more remarkable fluorescence response ((F660/F446)/(F660/F446)0) to LHU@MIP for the specificity of TNP-imprinted cavities. The results revealed that tailormade cavities of LHU@MIP were completely complementary with TNP in the size and shape.

To evaluate the stability of LHU@MIP, the fluorescence response of LHU@MIP was investigated. As presented in Fig. S7A, the values ((F660/F446)/(F660/F446)0) of LHU@MIP displayed no significant changes, manifesting that LHU@MIP possessed excellent fluorescence stability. To evaluate the repeatability of the developed sensors, the fluorescence response of LHU@MIP with 20 μΜ of TNP was studied by testing eight samples. As shown in Fig. S7B, the fluorescence response ((F660/F446)/(F660/F446)0) of LHU@MIP displayed no significant changes The result indicated that the prepared sensor had good repeatability for TNP. In addition, five removal-rebinding cycles were executed to appraise the reusability of the LHU@MIP. As shown in Fig. S8A, the change of the values ((F660/F446)/(F660/F446)0) for the five removal-rebinding cycles was recorded. The value decreased slightly with the increase in the number of repetitions and remained at 94.23% of the original value. Meanwhile, the TEM images (Fig. S8B) showed that the nanosensor maintained the original core-shell structure and unique morphology after five cycles. This indicated that the nanosensor possessed ideal reusability. The above results indicated that LHU@MIP can be reused at least 5 times without significant loss of measurement capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TNP in river water, tap water and soil
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the feasibility of LHU@MIP in real samples, the river water, tap water and soil were selected as simulated real samples. Because TNP was not detected in the three samples, spiking and recovery experiments were executed by analyzing spiked river water, tap water and soil containing final TNP concentrations of 5 μM, 10 μΜ and 20 μM. As shown in Table 1 , The recoveries of TNP in river water, tap water and soil were 96.8–100.2%, 96.9–99.4% and 96.1–99.15%, respectively and the RSD was lower than 3.1%. The results indicated the constructed LHU@MIP sensor had satisfying reliability for the detection of TNP in river water, tap water and soil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing for real-time TNP detection
ce:section.ce:para: Visual analysis with the naked eye was not conducive to distinguishing subtle color changes that lead to individual visual errors. Therefore, a smartphone-based 3D printing equipment was constructed for on-site detection of TNP. As portrayed in Fig. S9, the equipment consisted of a UV lamp, a sample tank, an optical filter and a light-proof box. As depicted in Fig. 6 , the sensing process was displayed. Firstly, LHU@MIP contained different concentrations of TNP and incubated for 1 min. After, under the same parameter conditions, a series of fluorescence images were acquired by smartphone and further converted to RGB values by software (Color picker). The relationship of R/B and the TNP concentrations exhibited an excellent linear in the range of 2–80 μM, which was denoted by y = 0.0028 x + 0.7512 (R2 = 0.986). Meanwhile, according to the standard 3σ/k, the LOD was 1.73 μM. Compared to LHU@NIP, LHU@MIP showed obvious color changes from blue to green (Fig. S10). The results demonstrated that the as-prepared smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensor platform provided a reliable strategy for on-site analysis and detection of TNP.

Similarly, river water, soil and tap water were used to investigate the practicality of the smartphone-based sensing platform. The recovery rates were 93.2–99.1% with RSD <3.5%, as shown in Table 2 . The results indicated that the sensing platform achieved satisfactory results on the detection of TNP and this method had great potential for onsite detection of TNP.

In summary, a novel fluorescence sensor based on LHU@MIP was successfully fabricated and achieved sensitive and rapid detection of TNP. Combining the advantages of high quantum yields of Mg, N-CDs, stability of UiO-66, and high selectivity of molecularly imprinted, the prepared LHU@MIP-based sensors exhibit good linearity. And a portable smartphone sensing device was developed. Significantly, the smartphone-assisted fluorescence molecularly imprinted sensor can fast, simple, and accurate on-site analysis of TNP in the simulated real samples with satisfactory results. In short, this dual-response method based on smartphone offers a potential model for the development of portable TNP sensors.

Lidong Yang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing- Original draft preparation. Wei Hu: Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Software. Fubin Pei: Validation. Bin Du: Formal Analysis, Investigation. Zhaoyang Tong: Software, Resources. Xihui Mu: Supervision, Resources. Mingzhu Xia: Resources. Fengyun Wang: Project administration, Writing - Review & Editing. Bing Liu: Project administration, Writing - Review & Editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian (SKLNBC2022-06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following the supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2023.125115.
